chapter_summary: Chapter 4 argues that the American elementary curriculum has been profoundly diluted not just by high-stakes testing, but by the triumph of three flawed ideas: developmentalism, individualism, and skill-centrism. The author contends that moving toward a knowledge-rich curriculum is the only way to resolve systemic failures like preschool fadeout and the achievement gap.

claims.text: High-stakes testing introduced by No Child Left Behind (NCLB) in 2002 caused a reduction in the knowledge students gained in history, civics, music, visual art, literature, and science.
claims.source_passage: It is widely conceded that the high-stakes testing introduced by No Child Left Behind (NCLB) in 2002 caused a reduction in the knowledge students have gained in history, civics, music, visual art, literature, and science.

claims.text: There has been a 47 percent reduction in class time devoted to subjects beyond math and reading since the implementation of NCLB.
claims.source_passage: In one study, the researcher noted that there had been a 47 percent reduction in class time devoted to subjects beyond math and reading.

claims.text: The impact of the narrow testing regime has been particularly devastating for students in low-income communities.
claims.source_passage: For students in low-income communities, the impact has been devastating.

claims.text: Curricular dilution in elementary schools is caused by fundamental guiding ideas rather than just high-stakes tests.
claims.source_passage: But that is only part of the curriculum-narrowing story. In this chapter I will probe more deeply into the causes of curricular dilution in the elementary school. These go beyond the influence of high-stakes tests, and pertain to fundamental guiding ideas—including an unproductive emphasis on skills rather than on knowledge.

claims.text: Imparting a well-rounded, knowledge-based curriculum is the solution to preschool fadeout and the achievement gap.
claims.source_passage: My positive, corrective thesis will be that imparting a well-rounded, knowledge-based curriculum will be the solution to many recalcitrant problems of our schooling, including preschool fadeout, unacceptable achievement gaps, and the tribulations of the Common Core State Standards.

claims.text: The current problems in American schooling are largely attributable to a diluted and aimless elementary curriculum emphasizing hands-on natural development.
claims.source_passage: All of these problems are largely attributable to a diluted and aimless elementary curriculum, emphasizing hands-on natural development and the child’s uniqueness.

claims.text: Child-centered curriculum principles were introduced in the 1930s and consolidated across the United States by the 1950s.
claims.source_passage: These curriculum principles were introduced in the 1930s, and consolidated throughout the land by the 1950s.

claims.text: In the child-centered model, the specific content through which general skills are imparted is deemed secondary.
claims.source_passage: The content through which those skills were to be imparted was deemed secondary.

claims.text: Modern 'twenty-first-century skills' are essentially the same as the 'twentieth-century skills' of the past, rebranded.
claims.source_passage: In the twentieth century these general skills were called “twentieth-century skills” and included critical thinking, problem solving, creativity, teamwork, and communication. These are now termed “twenty-first-century skills,” or “information age skills.”

claims.text: The immediate causes of the incoherent primary curriculum are the pedagogical principles of individualism and skill-centrism.
claims.source_passage: The more immediate causes of our currently incoherent and diluted primary curriculum are the two pedagogical principles that arose from the natural-development idea: individualism and skill-centrism.

claims.text: Standardized tests must be based on skills because the lack of a specific curriculum makes content-based testing impossible.
claims.source_passage: In the absence of a specific curriculum, tests cannot be devised that are based on specific curricular content, so they must be based on skills.

claims.text: Jean Piaget's theories are currently used by educators to justify the delay of academic content based on 'developmental inappropriateness.'
claims.source_passage: Educators assert, for example, that Piaget has shown that some topics are “developmentally inappropriate” for children in a particular age group.

claims.text: Skills are domain-specific rather than general, meaning formal operations are performed better when tied to concrete experience.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, there is some indication that even in Western cultures people can do formal operations much better when they are tied to concrete experience—another version of the domain specificity of skills.

claims.text: Piaget's description of the first three stages of mental development (sensorimotor, preoperational, and concrete operational) has held up, with the qualification that the stages are not discrete.
claims.source_passage: But his description of the first three stages has held up, with the main qualification being that the stages are not discrete.

claims.text: Piagetian developmental stages are highly subject-matter dependent.
claims.source_passage: They are very subject-matter dependent, just as with adults. Children (like adults) will be at a more advanced operational stage with more familiar topics.

claims.text: The fourth stage of Piaget's theory—formal operations—may be culturally determined rather than universal.
claims.source_passage: The anthropological field researchers who report this finding conclude that the fourth stage in Piaget’s theory may be culturally determined, not universal.

claims.text: Piaget's developmental stages are highly co-determined by schooling and culture rather than being a mark of physiological maturation alone.
claims.source_passage: Piaget came to agree that his stages were not simply a mark of physiological maturation and brain development, but were highly co-determined by schooling and culture.

claims.text: While the order of developmental stages is constant across societies, the chronological ages at which they are reached vary a great deal.
claims.source_passage: However, although the order of succession is constant, the chronological ages of these stages vary a great deal.

claims.text: Natural development does not determine educational readiness.
claims.source_passage: Instead of confirming the principle that natural development determines readiness, Piaget here outlines why that cannot be so.

claims.text: The difference in developmental progress between children in Montreal and Martinique is most likely caused by differences in their knowledge base and learning environments.
claims.source_passage: The most likely factor is the knowledge base of the children as determined by their learning environments at home, and in their schooling.

claims.text: External education, rather than internal 'unfolding,' explains why some children are developmentally ahead of or behind others.
claims.source_passage: It was external education, not internal unfolding, that explained why some children were ahead, and some behind.

claims.text: The idea that specific ages are inherently appropriate or inappropriate for particular academic topics has no scientific support.
claims.source_passage: as the eminent research psychologist Jerome Bruner once memorably observed, the idea that there are ages for which particular topics are appropriate has no scientific support.

claims.text: Educational factors, rather than maturational factors, are the primary cause of when children reach specific cognitive stages, such as the concrete operational stage.
claims.source_passage: Educational factors, not maturational factors, were the chief causes of the age when children reached the “concrete operational stage.”

claims.text: Human mental development is inherently dependent on culture and is not a purely natural or internal unfolding.
claims.source_passage: recent advances within developmental psychology have ascertained that the universal pathways of mental development inherently owe as much to culture as to nature.

claims.text: Any academic topic can be taught to any child in an appropriate mode if the teacher accounts for the student's current knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Such variability makes all the more important the tolerant Bruner principle that any topic can be taught appropriately, and provides a further reason to stop using the term “developmentally inappropriate” as a reason for delaying substantive content.

claims.text: Delaying substantive content like Mesopotamia in favor of 'age-appropriate' local topics puts disadvantaged students at a cumulative disadvantage.
claims.source_passage: to delay Mesopotamia is simply to put disadvantaged students at a further disadvantage compared to those first graders who for decades have been happily learning about Mesopotamia while their parents have read aloud to them from What Your First Grader Needs to Know.

claims.text: Academic readiness is determined by a child's prior knowledge rather than their biological age or developmental stage.
claims.source_passage: Appropriate topics are determined less by the child’s age than by what the child has already learned.

claims.text: Exposing children to high-level material they do not yet fully understand, such as the Pledge of Allegiance, causes no scientific or psychological harm.
claims.source_passage: To pretend that harm would somehow result is altogether baseless. First graders haven’t been harmed by being exposed to the Pledge of Allegiance just because they don’t yet quite understand it.

claims.text: The educational tradition of 'natural development' is factually incorrect and has caused the anti-intellectualism of modern American education.
claims.source_passage: The entire idea of natural development as it is conceived by the post-romantic tradition in education is incorrect, and must be vigorously rejected.

claims.text: Educational theorists have incorrectly assumed that tactile and visual learning are more 'natural' for children than abstract and verbal learning.
claims.source_passage: Somehow the idea became accepted among theorists, again without scientific support, that the tactile and visual are more natural than the abstract and verbal.

claims.text: The 'anti-transmission' doctrine in education holds back disadvantaged children by depriving them of the facts and abstractions their advantaged peers receive at home.
claims.source_passage: This has held back disadvantaged children differentially, because advantaged children are being read aloud to and are hearing plenty of words, facts, and abstractions at home.

claims.text: The transmission of shared, traditional knowledge is essential to human development and the realization of equal opportunity in a democracy.
claims.source_passage: Transmission, far from being in conflict with human development, is essential to it. And in a democracy... initiation into the shared knowledge of the standard language alone can make the ideal of equal opportunity a reality.

claims.text: The transmission of traditional knowledge is wrongly characterized by contemporary theorists as culturally imperialistic, conservative, and illiberal.
claims.source_passage: Transmission of traditional knowledge is said to be conformist and suppressive of individuality. This anti-transmission doctrine is probably the most astonishingly misguided principle yet devised under the natural-development conception.

claims.text: Initiation into shared knowledge of the standard language is a prerequisite for realizing the ideal of equal opportunity in a democracy.
claims.source_passage: And in a democracy, where each meets each on an equal footing, initiation into the shared knowledge of the standard language alone can make the ideal of equal opportunity a reality.

claims.text: Human mental development does not strictly follow physiological development.
claims.source_passage: Human mental development does not slavishly follow physiological development.

claims.text: Schools have a duty in a modern democracy to transmit the shared knowledge and cultural commons of the nation's public sphere.
claims.source_passage: In a modern democracy, whatever the home culture, the duty of the schools is to transmit the shared knowledge of the standard language—to transmit the cultural commons of the nation, its public sphere.

claims.text: The educational focus on hyper-individualism has resulted in curricular fragmentation.
claims.source_passage: The gradual dominance of the everyone-is-unique idea of education has resulted in curricular fragmentation.

claims.text: Content fragmentation in language arts classes is tolerated because educators incorrectly believe specific content is irrelevant to developing general reading skills.
claims.source_passage: Content fragmentation is tolerated because the particular content is felt to be somewhat irrelevant. The larger purpose is thought to be that of developing general reading skill while accommodating topics to students’ individual interests and abilities.

claims.text: It is practically impossible for a teacher to successfully individualize the content of a language arts class for 12 to 20 students without neglecting some students.
claims.source_passage: But no teacher can successfully individualize the content of a language arts class of twenty, or even twelve, without neglecting other students while doing so.

claims.text: Theories of multiple learning styles and multiple intelligences are not accepted by experts and have not passed scientific muster.
claims.source_passage: Neither “multiple learning styles” nor “multiple intelligences” is accepted by experts in the relevant fields.

claims.text: Instruction based on learning styles and multiple intelligences is not truly child-centered and perpetuates social inequality.
claims.source_passage: Paradoxically, our assumptions about the learning styles and types of “intelligence” of individual children have led to instruction that is far from child centered, and has perpetuated inequality among children from different backgrounds.

claims.text: Multiple-intelligences theories are excessively nativist, overemphasizing innate genetics while underestimating the role of shared knowledge in achievement.
claims.source_passage: One defect of multiple-intelligences theories is that they are far too nativist; that is, they overstress the innate, genetic component of learn- ing and understress the importance of relevant shared knowledge in a child’s ability to reach a high level of achievement.

claims.text: Child-centered education carries an implicit danger of genetic determinism regarding both ability and interest.
claims.source_passage: Inside the developmental conception of education has always lurked the danger of genetic determinism. In the naturalistic and individualistic character of child-centered education there has always been an implicit overemphasis on inborn ability as well as inborn interest.

claims.text: The developmental conception of education carries an inherent risk of promoting genetic determinism.
claims.source_passage: Inside the develop- mental conception of education has always lurked the danger of genetic determinism.

claims.text: Child-centered education has historically overemphasized inborn ability and interest.
claims.source_passage: In the naturalistic and individualistic character of child- centered education there has always been an implicit overemphasis on inborn ability as well as inborn interest

claims.text: Modern theories of learning styles and multiple intelligences are merely updated versions of the long-standing individualistic principle in American education.
claims.source_passage: The recent theories of “learning styles” and “multiple intelligences” fulfilled that established need for a constantly updated version of the individualistic principle.

claims.text: Multiple-intelligences theory lacks scientific evidence and provides no practical educational utility.
claims.source_passage: But as George A. Miller observed in his review of the mul- tiple-intelligences idea, it is not only underevidenced scientifically, it is also unhelpful educationally.

claims.text: Effective educational decisions require a clear definition of curriculum mastery rather than reliance on psychological theories of intelligence.
claims.source_passage: For any of these educational decisions one does not need the sanction of a questionable psychological theory. One needs a firm conception of what the child needs to master under this year’s defined curriculum.

claims.text: A fixed view of intelligence discourages student progress, whereas believing effort increases intelligence actually improves outcomes.
claims.source_passage: Carol Dweck’s work on “mindset” has shown that taking a fixed view of intelligence discourages progress, whereas a belief that hard work will make you smarter can indeed, with hard work, make you smarter.

claims.text: Schools must support student effort by providing a specific, cumulative curriculum based on enabling knowledge.
claims.source_passage: But the school has to do its part by making the hard work productive through a specific, cumulative curriculum based on the acquisition of enabling knowledge.

claims.text: Educational performance and fairness have not been improved by curricula based on theories of individuality.
claims.source_passage: The incoherent classrooms and curricula supported by these scien- tifically challenged theories of individuality have not worked to improve either academic performance or fairness.

claims.text: Many observed differences in learning ability are actually differences in the student's prior topic-relevant knowledge.
claims.source_passage: There is a growing body of evidence that many practical differences in students’ ability to learn the academic curriculum are differences in the topic-relevant knowledge that they already possess.

claims.text: Nations that successfully narrow achievement gaps utilize a multiyear arc of commonly learned subject matter in elementary grades.
claims.source_passage: That goal was achieved best by systems that followed a long, multiyear arc of commonly learned subject matter in the elementary grades.

claims.text: Differentiated instruction (DI) dumbs down education and impedes effective teaching by creating logistical chaos in the classroom.
claims.source_passage: And it dumbed down instruction: In English, “creative” students made things or drew pictures; “analytical” students got to read and write. In these ways, Differentiated Instruction, or DI, corrupted both curriculum and effective instruction.

claims.text: Differentiated Instruction (DI) often devolves into a collection of low-quality worksheets, coloring exercises, and specious 'kinesthetic' activities.
claims.source_passage: The attempt often devolved into a frantically assembled collection of worksheets, coloring exercises, and specious “kinesthetic” activities.

claims.text: Differentiated Instruction (DI) dumbs down instruction by assigning creative tasks to some students while reserving reading and writing for others.
claims.source_passage: And it dumbed down instruction: In English, “creative” students made things or drew pictures; “analytical” students got to read and write.

claims.text: The logistical demands of teaching multiple groups simultaneously make it nearly impossible to provide sustained, high-quality lessons for every child.
claims.source_passage: With so many groups to teach, instructors found it almost impossible to provide sustained, properly executed lessons for every child or group—and in a single class period.

claims.text: Classroom techniques like group work and independent seatwork are often used to hide the fact that most students are not receiving the teacher's attention during individualized instruction.
claims.source_passage: All sorts of techniques conspire to obscure that fact—group work, isolated seatwork on boring work sheets, and “independent study” with choice of books from the leveled-reader bin.

claims.text: Standard school libraries with high-quality books have declined as haphazard leveled classroom libraries have proliferated.
claims.source_passage: While haphazard leveled classroom libraries have grown, regular school libraries with real books have languished.

claims.text: There is no empirical evidence that individualizing reading through leveled-reader bins develops deep student interest or improves reading ability.
claims.source_passage: There’s no evidence that the individualization of leveled-reader bins develops deep student interest or makes better readers.

claims.text: Systematic knowledge building is the primary driver of high reading ability.
claims.source_passage: On the contrary, it neglects systematic knowledge building—the key to high reading ability.

claims.text: Solicitude for a child's individuality in reading selection leads to subject-matter incoherence and long-term educational neglect of the individual.
claims.source_passage: In effect it’s a perfect example of how solicitude for the individuality of the child leads to subject-matter incoherence and, in a larger, long-range view, to individual neglect.

claims.text: Acquiring real knowledge is what actually fosters genuine interest and individuality in students.
claims.source_passage: On the contrary, real knowledge breeds real interest and individuality.

claims.text: The United States is currently failing to improve reading comprehension for disadvantaged students as effectively as nations that avoid hyper-individualistic methods.
claims.source_passage: But I ask them to consider that right now in the United States we are not improving the reading comprehension of disadvantaged students as well as other nations that don’t follow these hyper-individualistic methods.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is not a general, transferable skill that can be developed using any arbitrary text.
claims.source_passage: reading comprehension is not a general skill that can be developed through any appealing vehicle

claims.text: The best way to ensure long-term reading comprehension is to ensure high oral comprehension in the earliest grades.
claims.source_passage: It’s been demonstrated that the very best way to ensure high reading comprehension in the long term is to ensure high oral comprehension in the earliest grades.

claims.text: Effective expansion of student knowledge and vocabulary requires that children in a class are 'on the same page' most of the time.
claims.source_passage: Basic arithmetic says that this can only happen effectively when most of the time children are on the same page.

claims.text: A 'semantic slippage' has occurred where 'independent reading' is now incorrectly defined exclusively as student-selected reading.
claims.source_passage: A semantic slippage occurred with the term “independent reading.” It is now taken to mean “reading books selected by the students themselves.”

claims.text: Early phonics training (decoding) should be taught separately from high-substance content to avoid taxing students' cognitive resources.
claims.source_passage: That’s true, but it’s best to separate early phonics training from taxing substance.

claims.text: The transition from communal whole-class discussion to individualized silent reading of assigned books is a 'semantic slippage' with significant negative consequences for education.
claims.source_passage: This small semantic slippage has had big consequences. One can see how it happened. Under child-centered principles, it would seem malpractice to have all the children in a class read the very same title, and then discuss the contents. Under a communal view of education, such whole-class activity would seem the most natural thing in the world.

claims.text: Whole-class activity is the most lively and productive kind of classroom environment.
claims.source_passage: It is by far the most lively and productive kind of classroom.

claims.text: Phonics training for early decoding should be kept separate from the study of 'taxing' or substantial subject matter.
claims.source_passage: That’s true, but it’s best to separate early phonics training from taxing substance.

claims.text: Listening to and discussing substantial books as a class is superior to individualized reading for building comprehension in early grades.
claims.source_passage: That’s why listening to and discussing substantial books in class is one element of a better approach.

claims.text: The 'leveled reader' system serves as a technical device to satisfy the ideological imperative to individualize the curriculum based on child interest and ability.
claims.source_passage: The knotty problem of choosing the subject matters of the language arts curriculum is thus partly solved by a technical difficulty–rating device that satisfies the imperative to individualize the curriculum to suit each child’s interests and abilities.

claims.text: In the current leveled-reader system, the child, rather than the teacher or school, effectively makes the final curricular decisions.
claims.source_passage: And the technical beauty of the whole system is that the only person who has to make the final curricular decision is the child.

claims.text: Reading comprehension is incorrectly taught to teachers as an 'all-purpose skill.'
claims.source_passage: Teachers are being blamed for the poor results of this system. But our teachers have been misinformed about the actual nature of reading comprehension. They have been told, incorrectly, that it is an all-purpose skill.

claims.text: The actual difficulty of a text for a specific student depends on their topic-specific knowledge, not just general complexity metrics.
claims.source_passage: Actual difficulty, not theoretical difficulty, is what counts. A student can be an excellent reader about dinosaurs and a terrible reader about mushrooms; the leveled-reader system is not individualistic in the one respect that it needs to be.

claims.text: Standard text-leveling formulas (like Lexile) focus on sentence length and word rarity while ignoring the reader's familiarity with the topic.
claims.source_passage: The formulas are mainly interested in lengths of sentences and rarity of words. The actual difficulty of a book is highly dependent on an individual student’s familiarity with the topic, whereas the formulas that determine a book’s difficulty level are quite indifferent to topics and meanings.

claims.text: The mathematical precision of text-leveling formulas (carrying out to five decimal places) is a 'pseudo-precision' used to dazzle educators.
claims.source_passage: The five decimal places speciously imply an exactitude of one in one hundred thousand. Teachers are being lulled into premature dazzlement by this pseudo-precision.

claims.text: The leveled-reader system lacks a firm scientific basis and lacks evidence of success.
claims.source_passage: Professor Tim Shanahan has shown that the whole leveled-reader edifice has no firm scientific basis and no evidence of success.

claims.text: Systematic content instruction is a more efficient tool for vocabulary building in the early grades than independent reading.
claims.source_passage: re powerful tools for reading progress, and are more efficient for vocabulary building in the early grades than independent reading is

claims.text: A systematic approach to content that includes all students in the class is the most effective method for students to gain new knowledge, interests, and skills.
claims.source_passage: A systematic approach to content that includes all students in the language arts class, and prepares them for knowledge to come, is the most effective method for all students to gain new knowledge—and with it, new interests and skills.

claims.text: Whole-class instruction where all students are deeply engaged provides more effective individual attention than individualized instruction.
claims.source_passage: Successful whole-class instruction in which all students are deeply engaged is a way of giving more attention to each child.

claims.text: Heterogeneous leveled readers on arbitrary topics are ineffective at helping students gain new knowledge, interests, and skills.
claims.source_passage: Heterogeneous leveled readers on arbitrary topics can’t accomplish that.

claims.text: Maintaining student attention is significantly easier when all students are learning the same subject matter and reading the same books.
claims.source_passage: Keeping everyone’s attention is accomplished readily if the students are learning the same subject matters and reading the same books.

claims.text: The effort to individualize the elementary curriculum has led to educational incoherence and a failure to reach all students.
claims.source_passage: In sum, the effort to individualize the elementary curriculum, especially in language arts, has led to incoherence and, paradoxically, to a failure in reaching all students.

claims.text: Individualization of the curriculum contributes to the deterioration of the school's primary socializing aim.
claims.source_passage: It has contributed to a deterioration in the primary socializing aim of the school.

claims.text: Neglecting coherent subject-matter learning over multiple years sustains the achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: It has helped sustain the gaps between advantaged and disadvantaged students by neglecting the long arc of coherent subject-matter learning and vocabulary building over the years.

claims.text: The theory of all-purpose skills is used as a justification for a fragmented and haphazard curriculum.
claims.source_passage: That has continued to be the theory set forth by school systems to justify the fragmented curriculum.

claims.text: School mission statements use thinking-skill rhetoric as a convenient substitute for clearly defined content in history, literature, and science.
claims.source_passage: While these statements are certainly believed by those making them, they are also convenient things to say to the public when it hasn’t been clearly determined what students will be learning in history, literature, music, art, science, and civics.

claims.text: The availability of online information does not eliminate the requirement for the memorization of facts.
claims.source_passage: In a world where most information is instantly available online, the mass memorization of facts is no longer required. ... Let me now turn to the reasons why that attractive idea has not worked and cannot work.

claims.text: Educational reformers and economists wrongly advocate for 'twenty-first-century skills' as a means to maintain global economic competitiveness.
claims.source_passage: To this chorus of educators must be added the chorus of reformers who continue to urge schools and the public that we must improve our students’ critical-thinking, problem-solving, and teamwork intelligence if the United States is to remain competitive in the new global economy.

claims.text: Reformers and educators prioritize critical thinking, curiosity, and creativity over the memorization of content in response to the new academic standards.
claims.source_passage: ‘All of that fits into the nationwide move to new academic standards, which will embrace learning across all subjects, and have students practice critical thinking, curiosity and creativity instead of merely memorizing content,’ Cash said.

claims.text: Reformers argue that improving critical-thinking, problem-solving, and teamwork is necessary for the United States to remain competitive in the global economy.
claims.source_passage: reformers who continue to urge schools and the public that we must improve our students’ critical-thinking, problem-solving, and teamwork intelligence if the United States is to remain competitive in the new global economy.

claims.text: Communication skills are central to and necessary for all other twenty-first-century skills, including teamwork and learning new tasks.
claims.source_passage: Everybody in these discussions should agree that verbal skills are central to all the twenty-first-century skills. . . Verbal skills are necessary in the twenty-first century to teamwork and learning new tasks.

claims.text: Effective verbal skills depend on a large vocabulary and an initiation into the language community of the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Effective verbal skills depend on a big vocabulary and an effective initiation into the language community of the public sphere—something that can only be gained by a good communal education, and essential to “communication skills.”

claims.text: Skill-centrism cannot succeed if it is pursued without the development of communication skills.
claims.source_passage: save the schools from content-indeterminate skill-centrism—for skill-­centrism on its own, without communication skills, cannot succeed.

claims.text: National NAEP reading comprehension scores for seventeen-year-olds serve as critical indices of the nation's achievement of twenty-first-century skills.
claims.source_passage: Those NAEP verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds are critical indices to how well we are achieving all twenty-first-century skills.

claims.text: In a modern democracy, literacy entails the ability to communicate with strangers and to understand complex texts.
claims.source_passage: In a modern democracy, literacy entails the ability to communicate effectively with strangers, and to understand rather complex texts.

claims.text: Mastery of the standard national language requires knowledge of a wide range of irrational idioms and unspoken shared connotations.
claims.source_passage: This language mastery entails not just a big vocabulary but also a big range of irrational idioms and unspoken shared connotations.

claims.text: Critical thinking does not exist as a domain-general construct distinct from general cognitive ability.
claims.source_passage: The report stated that there’s “little evidence that critical thinking exists as a domain-general construct distinct from general cognitive ability.”

claims.text: Human skills are domain specific and do not transfer readily from one subject area to another.
claims.source_passage: Cognitive scientists have known since the 1940s that human skills tend to be domain specific, and do not transfer readily from one domain to the next.

claims.text: When individuals confront unfamiliar content, their skills—including reading and writing—degenerate.
claims.source_passage: No matter how widely skilled people may be, as soon as they confront unfamiliar content their skill degenerates. That is true even of reading and writing skill.

claims.text: The idea that information is instantly available online has intensified anti-fact sentiments in education.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, the view that information is “instantly available online,” has intensified the antifact sentiments.

claims.text: A well-stocked mind is the foundational 'skill of skills' and is essential for both critical thinking and the ability to look things up.
claims.source_passage: The news needs to be spread far and wide: a well-stocked mind is the skill of skills—essential to critical thinking and to looking things up.

claims.text: Looking things up is a completely unsatisfactory procedure compared to having knowledge already present in a well-stocked mind.
claims.source_passage: It focused on work by the late George A. Miller that showed that looking things up was a completely unsatisfactory procedure compared to having a well-stocked mind.

claims.text: Both researchers and students recognize significant limitations and distractions when using online resources to find information.
claims.source_passage: still in 2015, both researchers and students themselves are “keenly aware of the limitations” and “distractions” of using online dictionaries, encyclopedias, and hand-held devices to look things up.

claims.text: Both researchers and students in the digital age are aware of the limitations and distractions inherent in using online reference tools.
claims.source_passage: I checked the literature and found that indeed, still in 2015, both researchers and students themselves are “keenly aware of the limitations” and “distractions” of using online dictionaries, encyclopedias, and hand-held devices to look things up.

claims.text: Children often experience frustration when looking up words because they lose the original context of their reading before they can construe the meaning.
claims.source_passage: In the time it took children to find a dictionary word and construe its meanings, they usually forgot the original context and never found their way back. They mainly experienced frustration.

claims.text: Uninformed children cannot effectively use dictionaries because they lack the background knowledge to choose between multiple ambiguous word definitions.
claims.source_passage: A young, uninformed child, faced (as Google Translate is) with ambiguous possibilities, cannot know which of the meaning possibilities are in play.

claims.text: Students will refuse to read if the text requires them to look up too many unknown words.
claims.source_passage: Psychologists have found that students simply will not read at all if they have to look up too many words.

claims.text: Looking up words in a dictionary is a slow, cumbersome, and less accurate method of vocabulary building compared to implicit word learning.
claims.source_passage: It has two defects: it is a slow, cumbersome mode of vocabulary building compared with implicit word learning, and it is a far less accurate mode of gaining the subtle connotations of words.

claims.text: Understanding the information found when 'looking things up' requires the same amount of background knowledge as any other form of language use.
claims.source_passage: The language of what is being looked up is like language use anywhere else. To understand it requires a lot of relevant background knowledge.

claims.text: Experts learn more and learn faster than novices when using reference tools because they only need to process one or two novel features.
claims.source_passage: But in fact it’s the expert who learns more that is new, and learns it much faster. It’s extremely hard for a novice to learn very much in a reason- able time by looking things up.

claims.text: Novices are hindered in information seeking because the human mind can only assimilate three or four new items before working memory is exhausted.
claims.source_passage: One reason is that the human mind is able to assimilate only three or four new items before further ele- ments evaporate from working memory.

claims.text: The internet and search engines like Google consolidate educational inequality by rewarding individuals who already possess background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Google is not an equal-opportunity fact finder; it rewards those already in the know. Instead of being an agent of equality, Google rewards cognitive insiders. It consolidates educational inequality.

claims.text: Educational progress is governed by the 'Matthew Effect,' where those who have knowledge gain more and those who lack it fall further behind.
claims.source_passage: Education is bootstrapping, governed by the “Matthew Effect,” that unfair paradox in the Book of Matthew: “For whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and he shall have more abundance: but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath.”

claims.text: There is no consistent all-purpose problem-solving skill that exists independently of domain-specific knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The skill of problem solving is also dependent on domain-specific knowledge. There exists no consistent all-purpose problem-solving skill, independent of domain-specific knowledge.

claims.text: Critical thinking is actually either a set of very specific reasoning skills or the manifestation of expertise in a specific field.
claims.source_passage: Instead, what people call critical thinking is either a class of very specific reasoning skills, or the formation of expertise in a field (e.g., medicine, accounting).

claims.text: The process of looking information up is significantly less efficient than having knowledge in working memory.
claims.source_passage: the process of looking things up is far more cumbersome than having a well-stocked mind that quickly populates working memory with the necessary elements before it “forgets the original context and never finds its way back.”

claims.text: Listening ability in early grades is the primary key to later reading ability.
claims.source_passage: That’s why listening ability in early grades is the key to reading ability later on.

claims.text: Reading aloud to children and classroom discussion should play a big role in the early grades.
claims.source_passage: Reading aloud to children and classroom discussion should play a big role in the early grades.

claims.text: Decoding is now being better taught in the United States, which accounts for the rise in third-grade reading scores.
claims.source_passage: Decoding is now being better taught in the United States, which accounts for the rise in third-grade reading scores.

claims.text: Decoding is a skill that applies to all texts in standard written English.
claims.source_passage: Decoding is a skill that applies to all texts in standard written English, though of course it is domain specific to that language and its standard spellings.

claims.text: Reading comprehension, unlike decoding, is not a general skill for standard written English.
claims.source_passage: But reading comprehension, unlike decoding, is not a general skill for standard written English.

claims.text: Comprehension involves making hypotheses about what the words mean.
claims.source_passage: Comprehension involves making hypotheses about what the words mean.

claims.text: The No Child Left Behind Act and the Common Core initiative were dominated by the incorrect assumption that reading could be taught and tested as a general skill.
claims.source_passage: Two assumptions about reading dominated the current No Child Left Behind Act, and to some extent the Common Core initiative: first, that incentives and sanctions would change teachers’ behavior; and second, that reading could be taught and tested as a general skill.

claims.text: Mature reading comprehension did not improve under NCLB because comprehension cannot be taught as a general skill.
claims.source_passage: But mature reading comprehension did not improve, because the assumption that reading comprehension can be taught as a general skill is incorrect.

claims.text: A well-rounded education is the only means of attaining all-around reading skill.
claims.source_passage: That’s why a well-rounded education is the best means of attaining all-around reading skill. Actually, it’s the only means of doing so.

claims.text: Most of the information needed to understand a text is not provided by the text itself but must be drawn from the reader's prior knowledge.
claims.source_passage: One of the major contributions of psychology is the recognition that much of the information needed to understand a text is not provided by the information expressed in the text itself but must be drawn from the language user’s knowledge of the person, objects, states of affairs, or events the discourse is about.

claims.text: Language is surrounded by a 'penumbra' of unspoken, shared knowledge without which speech and text cannot be understood.
claims.source_passage: Language is surrounded by a cloud of taken-for-granted, unsaid knowledge, without which the said cannot be understood. This penumbra of unspoken knowledge that surrounds speech, and makes it comprehensible, is the knowledge possessed by well-educated insiders in a speech community.

claims.text: Topic-relevant domain knowledge trumps basic cognitive differences such as IQ in determining reading performance.
claims.source_passage: it showed the extent to which topic-relevant knowledge trumps basic cognitive differences. The low-IQ students who knew a lot about soccer outperformed the high-IQ students who had little soccer knowledge.

claims.text: When a topic is unfamiliar, reading comprehension is degraded regardless of the reader's general reading ability.
claims.source_passage: When the topic is unfamiliar, the reader can’t contribute information; the situation model is fuzzy at best and comprehension is degraded.

claims.text: Reading comprehension strategies cannot provide the necessary information required to understand a text on an unfamiliar topic.
claims.source_passage: Mere strategies cannot provide the necessary information.

claims.text: When a reading topic is unfamiliar, the performance gap between typically 'better' readers and 'poor' readers significantly narrows or disappears.
claims.source_passage: when a topic is unfamiliar, normally better readers lose their advantage.

claims.text: The meaning of a text is constructed by combining presented information with the reader's existing knowledge to form a situation model.
claims.source_passage: The meaning of a text is constructed from both presented information and reader-contributed information to form the needed situation model.

claims.text: Reading strategies cannot compensate for a lack of topic-specific information when a reader attempts to comprehend a text.
claims.source_passage: Mere strategies cannot provide the necessary information.

claims.text: Systematic knowledge gain is a primary tool for serving educational equity for both economically and cognitively disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Equity is served by systematic knowledge gain both among the economically and the cognitively disadvantaged.

claims.text: Reading comprehension depends less on the technical complexity of a text than on the reader's familiarity with the subject matter.
claims.source_passage: comprehension of texts depends less on text complexity than on the reader’s familiarity with the topic.

claims.text: Domain knowledge facilitates verbal comprehension because reading is essentially a form of problem solving.
claims.source_passage: verbal comprehension is a form of problem solving. Topic familiarity makes the task of forming a situation model fast and accurate, leaving more space in working memory to figure out word relations, whether they are complex or simple.

claims.text: The most effective way to teach 'problem-solving skill' is to provide students with a broad, knowledge-rich education.
claims.source_passage: Domain knowledge facilitates problem solving in any domain—hence, the best way to teach “problem-solving skill” is to offer a broad education!

claims.text: A reader's ability to accurately guess the meaning of rare words and complex sentences increases with their prior knowledge of the topic.
claims.source_passage: Text complexity is shrugged off and unfamiliar words are rightly guessed when the topic is familiar.

claims.text: Treating reading as a general, content-independent skill is a failed educational paradigm that yields little long-term benefit.
claims.source_passage: The mistaken conception of reading as a general skill has led to a frantic short-term, quick-fix view of education that has resulted in little long-term payoff

claims.text: The narrowing of the elementary curriculum to focus on reading techniques has diminished the long-term vocabulary and communication skills of students.
claims.source_passage: The narrowing of the elementary school curriculum that ensued through the increased focus on technique over knowledge gain has diminished knowledge and vocabularies, and thus the communication skills of older students.

claims.text: A substantive, coherent elementary curriculum from preschool through grade five is the only proven method to ensure high-level verbal competence for all students in a nation.
claims.source_passage: On the basis of the evidence and the research to date in the year 2016, such a curriculum is the only means yet devised to bring nearly all students in a nation to adequate competence, and a large percentage to very high competence, in reading and writing.

claims.text: Effective teaching is an empathetic activity that depends fundamentally on the instructor's understanding of where the student currently stands in relation to the teaching goal.
claims.source_passage: Effective teaching is an empathetic activity that always depends on where the student is with respect to the teaching goal.

claims.text: The phrase 'developmentally inappropriate' is used as a tool for 'sanctimonious bullying' to keep interesting and high-level knowledge away from disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: The only important real-world effect of such sanctimonious bullying is to withhold from disadvantaged students high-level material that is widely known to advantaged ones.

claims.text: The post-1940s acceptance of anti-verbalism and anti-intellectualism in American education is based on the unscientific assumption that tactile and visual learning are more 'natural' than abstract and verbal learning.
claims.source_passage: It has also been the cause of the unfortunate anti-verbalism and anti-intellectualism of post-1940 American education. Somehow the idea became accepted among theorists, again without scientific support, that the tactile and visual are more natural than the abstract and verbal.

claims.text: Human development inherently requires social inculcation and communal transmission; growing up without a community is 'totally unnatural' for a child.
claims.source_passage: But human development absolutely requires social inculcation as part of a child’s natural development. It’s totally unnatural for a child to grow up without a community.

claims.text: The transmission of communal knowledge is a biological and social universal as natural as mother's milk.
claims.source_passage: The transmission of communal knowledge is as natural as mother's milk.

claims.text: Differentiated Instruction (DI) prevents teachers from employing the 'protean, decades-old elements of a good lesson' by fragmenting the classroom into unmanageable sub-groups.
claims.source_passage: It profoundly impeded the teacher’s ability to incorporate those protean, decades-old elements of a good lesson.

claims.text: Reformers and educators should unite around a knowledge-based curriculum by recognizing that verbal skills are foundational to all twenty-first-century skills, including teamwork and communication.
claims.source_passage: All the participants in these discussions should therefore quickly seize upon that point about communication skills, emphasize it strongly, and then go forward to reach a productive agreement. We could start by agreeing on the need for a coherent knowledge-based elementary curriculum.

claims.text: Experts learn faster and more effectively from reference tools like the Internet because their existing knowledge allows them to pay attention only to one or two novel features that integrate easily into memory.
claims.source_passage: Experts have already assimilated most of the elements being looked up, and therefore need pay attention only to one or two novel features that can easily be integrated into their prior knowledge.

claims.text: The Duncker radiation experiment demonstrates that structural problem-solving methods do not naturally transfer from one domain to another.
claims.source_passage: The famous Duncker radiation experiment showed that even when people are carefully shown how to solve a problem in one domain, they will tend to be baffled by that same structural problem when it is set in another domain.

claims.text: Reading ability serves as a rough proxy for all-around verbal expertise, encompassing writing, speaking, and listening skills.
claims.source_passage: Reading ability serves as a rough proxy for verbal expertise generally, including effective writing, speaking, and listening.

evidence.description: A study showing a 47% reduction in class time for subjects beyond math and reading during the NCLB era.
evidence.source_reference: Jennifer McMurrer, Choices, Changes, and Challenges (2007)
evidence.source_passage: In one study, the researcher noted that there had been a 47 percent reduction in class time devoted to subjects beyond math and reading.

evidence.description: Article documenting the disappearance of art, music, and PE due to high-stakes testing regimes.
evidence.source_reference: Tim Walker, 'The Testing Obsession and the Disappearing Curriculum,' NEA Today (2014)
evidence.source_passage: The law is uniformly blamed for stripping curriculum opportunities, including art, music, physical education and more...

evidence.description: Historical documentation of the failure of child-centered reforms and their consolidation in the mid-20th century.
evidence.source_reference: Diane Ravitch, Left Back: A Century of Failed School Reforms (2000)
evidence.source_passage: These curriculum principles were introduced in the 1930s, and consolidated throughout the land by the 1950s.

evidence.description: Piaget's observational study of his own children leading to the identification of four stages of mental development.
evidence.source_reference: Jean Piaget (1896–1980)
evidence.source_passage: He detected four stages in their mental development: the “sensorimotor stage” from infancy to age two, the “preoperational stage” from ages two to seven, the “concrete operational stage” from seven to eleven, and the “formal operational stage” in adolescence and maturity.

evidence.description: Anthropological research showing that adults in some cultures prioritize understanding human motivations and power over the logic of formal operations.
evidence.source_reference: Maynard and Greenfield, 'Implicit Cognitive Development in Cultural Tools and Children' (2003)
evidence.source_passage: “Who knows?” say highly able adults in some cultures, where the mark of maturity is not the manipulation of formal operations but the understanding of human motivations, personalities, and power relationships.

evidence.description: A study showing a two-year delay in children reaching developmental stages in rural Iranian villages compared to cities.
evidence.source_reference: Jean Piaget, 'Cognitive Development in Children' (1964)
evidence.source_passage: In Iran, furthermore, in the city of Teheran, they found approximately the same ages as we found in Geneva, but there is a systematic delay of two years in the children in the country.

evidence.description: A comparison between children in Montreal and Martinique using the same curriculum, showing a four-year developmental delay in Martinique.
evidence.source_reference: Monique Laurendeau and Father Adrien Pinard, cited in Piaget (1964)
evidence.source_passage: Canadian psychologists who redid our experiments... found a delay of four years in all the experiments and this in spite of the fact that the children in Martinique go to a school set up according to the French system.

evidence.description: World Health Organization data confirming no protein or vitamin deficiencies in Martinique, ruling out malnutrition as a cause for developmental delays.
evidence.source_reference: Pan American Health Organization (PAHO), 2012
evidence.source_passage: The World Health Organization does not report child malnutrition in Martinique: “There have been no reports of disorders associated with protein or vitamin deficiencies.”

evidence.description: Comparison of children in Martinique and Montreal showing that schooling/home environment, not maturation, explains the onset of the concrete operational stage.
Jerome Bruner's hypothesis that any subject can be taught in an intellectually honest form to any child at any stage.
evidence.source_reference: Jerome Bruner, The Process of Education (1960)

evidence.description: Research showing that universal mental development is a cultural process as much as a natural one.
evidence.source_reference: Patricia M. Greenfield et al. (2003)

evidence.description: Public debate regarding the teaching of Mesopotamia to first graders in New York, used to illustrate how DAP is used to delay content.
evidence.source_reference: Syracuse Post-Standard (2013)

evidence.description: Evidence that the claim of 'harm' from developmentally inappropriate topics is a myth exploded by psychologists.
evidence.source_reference: Judith J. Carta et al. (1991)

evidence.description: A meta-analysis showing that theories of multiple intelligences and learning styles lack validating empirical support and concordance with neuroscience.
evidence.source_reference: Lynn Waterhouse, 'Inadequate Evidence for Multiple Intelligences Mozart Effect, and Emotional Intelligence Theories,' Educational Psychologist 41, no. 4 (2006): 247–255.
evidence.source_passage: One meta-analysis concludes as follows: “In the absence of adequate validating empirical support, and in the absence of concord with neuroscience findings, [such theories] should not be applied in education.”

evidence.description: Historical example of a 1917 language arts series that explicitly defined its purpose as creating a common stock of knowledge for socialization and unity.
evidence.source_reference: Franklin T. Baker and Ashley H. Thorndike, 'Introduction,' Everyday Classics (New York: Macmillan, 1917).
evidence.source_passage: A best-selling language arts series stated: “We have chosen what is common, established, almost proverbial... what, in brief, every child in the land ought to know, because it is good, and because other people know it.”

evidence.description: Theoretical critique by Antonio Gramsci regarding the flaws in child-centered 'spontaneous development.'
evidence.source_reference: Antonio Gramsci, Notebook 1, 1929, Quaderni del Carcere, vol. 1.
evidence.source_passage: Antonio Gramsci so wittily observed when he said that the new education conceived “the child’s brain as a ball of string that the teacher should help to unwind.”

evidence.description: George A. Miller's 1983 review of multiple intelligences in the New York Times Review of Books, stating the theory is underevidenced.
evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller, 'Varieties of Intelligence', 1983

evidence.description: Carol Dweck's research on 'mindset' showing that belief in malleable intelligence leads to better results than belief in fixed intelligence.
evidence.source_reference: Carol Dweck, Mindset (2007)

evidence.description: The 2012 PISA study analysis showing that nations with common subject-matter curricula best narrowed achievement gaps.
evidence.source_reference: OECD, Low-Performing Students (2016)

evidence.description: A 'report from the field' describing how differentiated instruction results in fragmented lessons and 'specious' activities.
evidence.source_reference: Mike Schmoker, 'When Pedagogic Fads Trump Priorities', Education Week, 2010

evidence.description: Research showing prior domain knowledge is a more significant predictor of learning new content than general aptitude.
evidence.source_reference: Beier & Ackerman (2005); Hall & Edmondson (1992)

evidence.description: Evidence that whole-class, concept-oriented instruction improves reading engagement and comprehension.
evidence.source_reference: John T. Guthrie et al. (2004)
evidence.source_passage: Increasing Reading Comprehension and Engagement Through Concept-Oriented Reading Instruction [CORI]

evidence.description: Demonstration that oral comprehension levels (auding) are the primary predictor of later reading comprehension.
evidence.source_reference: Thomas G. Sticht (1975)
evidence.source_passage: Auding and Reading: A Developmental Model

evidence.description: Critique of 'instructional level theory' (leveled books) showing that students can learn from texts at various difficulty levels and that the theory lacks evidence.
evidence.source_reference: Timothy Shanahan (2011)
evidence.source_passage: basically we have put way too much confidence in an unproven theory.

evidence.description: Data showing that regular school libraries have declined while classroom collections have increased.
evidence.source_reference: American Library Association (2010)
evidence.source_passage: The State of America’s Libraries

evidence.description: Analysis of popular reading programs (e.g., Houghton Mifflin) showing they consist of heterogeneous, non-domain-based content.
evidence.source_reference: E.D. Hirsch (The Knowledge Deficit)
evidence.source_passage: the contents are thoroughly heterogeneous, not domain based.

evidence.description: The Lexile formula (old version) which uses logs of mean sentence length and word rarity to calculate difficulty.
evidence.source_reference: Endnote 36

evidence.description: Research by Tim Shanahan concluding that instructional level theory and leveled books lack scientific evidence and efficacy.
evidence.source_reference: Shanahan, 'Rejecting Instructional Level Theory' (2011)

evidence.description: General reference to successful practices in 'good primary schools over the world' where students learn new things through vigorous discussion.
evidence.source_reference: Not specified beyond 'good primary schools over the world'

evidence.description: A survey of mission statements from various US school districts (Milwaukee, Oakland, Tucson, etc.) showing a uniform focus on skills over content.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch's random sample of district mission statements

evidence.description: The inaugural statement of the Santa Barbara Unified School District superintendent emphasizing critical thinking over content memorization in preparation for Common Core.
evidence.source_reference: Giana Magnoli, 'Superintendent Says Culture Will Change in Santa Barbara School District,' Santa Barbara Noozhawk, 2013

evidence.description: Popular reform literature that argues for the prioritization of problem-solving and teamwork for the global economy.
evidence.source_reference: Tony Wagner, The Global Achievement Gap

evidence.description: Tony Wagner's book 'The Global Achievement Gap' which advocates for 21st-century skills like problem-solving and teamwork.
NAEP verbal scores for seventeen-year-olds used as a proxy for the achievement of communication skills.
The idiom 'You beat me to the punch' as an example of unspoken shared connotations in the public sphere.
Cognitive science research dating back to the 1940s regarding domain specificity.
George A. Miller's work on the limitations of 'looking things up.'
evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller (2000)

evidence.description: Research by Levy and Steel (2015) regarding the functionality and distractions of electronic dictionaries.
evidence.source_reference: Levy and Steel (2015)

evidence.description: Research on the limitations and distractions of electronic language dictionaries for learners.
evidence.source_reference: Levy and Steel, 'Language Learner Perspectives on the Functionality and Use of Electronic Language Dictionaries' (2015)
evidence.source_passage: I checked the literature and found that indeed, still in 2015, both researchers and students themselves are “keenly aware of the limitations” and “distractions” of using online dictionaries

evidence.description: Studies showing children's frustration and loss of context when using paper dictionaries to find word meanings.
evidence.source_reference: George A. Miller and Patricia M. Gildea, 'How Children Learn Words' (1987)
evidence.source_passage: In the time it took children to find a dictionary word and construe its meanings, they usually forgot the original context and never found their way back.

evidence.description: Research on the cognitive distractions and 'delinkification' caused by hyperlinks in digital reading.
evidence.source_reference: Nicholas Carr, 'Experiments in Delinkification' (2010)
evidence.source_passage: Those who have done research in the digital age have reported similar frustration with the distractions of hy- perlinks.

evidence.description: Cognitive science research demonstrating that novices have difficulty learning from reference materials due to working memory constraints.
evidence.source_reference: Jill Larkin et al., 'Expert and Novice Performance in Solving Physics Problems' (1980)
evidence.source_passage: It’s extremely hard for a novice to learn very much in a reason- able time by looking things up.

evidence.description: The application of the 'Matthew Effect' to reading and literacy acquisition.
evidence.source_reference: Keith E. Stanovich, 'Matthew Effects in Reading' (1986)
evidence.source_passage: Education is bootstrapping, gov- erned by the “Matthew Effect,” that unfair paradox in the Book of Matthew

evidence.description: The Duncker radiation experiment showed that people cannot solve the same structural problem when it is moved to a different domain.
Adriaan de Groot's chess experiment showed that masters' superior memory was only for valid game positions, not random placements, indicating domain-specific knowledge.
A 2011 National Academy of Sciences report found little evidence for critical thinking as a general skill.
Psychologist Nathan R. Kuncel argues research does not support critical thinking as a universal skill independent of domain knowledge.
Third-grade reading score increases attributed to improved decoding instruction.
evidence.source_reference: N/A

evidence.description: The National Reading Panel of 2000 recommendations which included strategy instruction.
evidence.source_reference: National Reading Panel (2000)

evidence.description: The 'Situation Model' theory describing how readers construct meaning using prior knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Teun van Dijk and Walter Kintsch, Strategies of Discourse Comprehension (1983)

evidence.description: Study showing 'poor readers' with baseball knowledge outperformed 'good readers' without it when reading about baseball.
evidence.source_reference: Donna R. Recht and Lauren Leslie (1988)

evidence.description: Study demonstrating that topic-relevant soccer knowledge determines comprehension performance more than IQ.
evidence.source_reference: Wolfgang Schneider et al. (1989)

evidence.description: A study showing that low-IQ students with high soccer knowledge outperformed high-IQ students with low soccer knowledge on reading tasks related to soccer.
evidence.source_reference: Schneider et al. (1999/1989)
evidence.source_passage: The low-IQ students who knew a lot about soccer outperformed the high-IQ students who had little soccer knowledge. Moreover, when both low- and high-IQ students were soccer experts, the low-IQ students performed just as well as those with high-IQs!

evidence.description: Research showing that text complexity (syntactic and lexical) had little effect on comprehension when third graders were familiar with the topic (e.g., toothpaste, jelly beans) but significantly hindered them when the topic was unfamiliar (e.g., tree frogs, soil).
evidence.source_reference: Arya, Hiebert, and Pearson (2011)
evidence.source_passage: With topics unfamiliar to third graders, like tree frogs and soil, text complexity slowed reading and debased understanding. But when the topics were familiar, as with toothpaste and jelly beans, the complexity of the text had little effect on accuracy or speed of comprehension.

evidence.description: The theoretical framework explaining that meaning is constructed from text-provided info and reader-contributed info.
evidence.source_reference: Teun A. van Dijk and Walter Kintsch (1983)
evidence.source_passage: The Kintsch/van Dijk insight into “situation models” explains why this is so.

evidence.description: A study identifying that the domain specificity of skills means individuals perform formal operations significantly better when they are tied to concrete experience.
evidence.source_reference: Paul Sheldon Davies, James H. Fetzer, and Thomas R. Foster, 'Logical Reasoning and Domain Specificity,' Biology and Philosophy 10, no. 1 (1995): 1–37.

evidence.description: A historical example of early 20th-century progressive education theory that used biological comparisons of internal organs to argue that children's 'mental organs' require different nutrition (content) than adults.
evidence.source_reference: Nathan Oppenheim, The Development of the Child (1898)

evidence.description: Research demonstrating that the time required for experts to memorize new information (five seconds versus sixty) is heavily dependent on their prior domain-specific knowledge.
evidence.source_reference: Fernand Gobet and Herbert A. Simon, 'Five Seconds or Sixty? Presentation Time in Expert Memory,' Cognitive Science 24 (2000): 651–682.

evidence.description: An empirical study investigating how existing subject-matter knowledge directly influences both the interest levels and the recall ability of students.
evidence.source_reference: Patricia A. Alexander, Jonna M. Kulikowich, and Sharon K. Schulze, 'How Subject-Matter Knowledge Affects Recall and Interest,' American Educational Research Journal 31 (1994): 313–337.

evidence.description: Historical media evidence from 1936 documenting that thousands of American schools had already shifted focus toward individuality and critical thinking over specific content.
evidence.source_reference: March of Time newsreel (1936), cited in Hirsch endnote 41

evidence.description: Thorndike's research found that learning Latin did not provide general mental training, but rather only improved the specific ability to read Latin, supporting the concept of domain-specific learning.
evidence.source_reference: Edward Thorndike, cited in endnote 42

evidence.description: Experimental research by Gick and Holyoak on analogical problem solving, demonstrating the difficulties of transferring structural problem-solving methods between different contexts.
evidence.source_reference: M. L. Gick and K. J. Holyoak, 'Analogical Problem Solving' (1980)

evidence.description: Neuroscience research identifying domain-specificity within elemental brain processors during visual perspective-taking tasks.
evidence.source_reference: Matthias Schurz et al., 'Clarifying the Role of Theory of Mind Areas During Visual Perspective Taking' (2015)

evidence.description: Research by Helfenstein and Saariluoma exploring the role of mental contents in the transfer of skills between different domains.
evidence.source_reference: Sacha Helfenstein and Pertti Saariluoma, 'Mental Contents in Transfer' (2006)

evidence.description: A study demonstrating that the effects of syntactic and lexical complexity on reading comprehension are secondary to the reader's familiarity with the subject matter in elementary science texts.
evidence.source_reference: Diana J. Arya, Elfrieda H. Hiebert, and P. David Pearson, 'The Effects of Syntactic and Lexical Complexity on the Comprehension of Elementary Science Texts,' International Electronic Journal of Elementary Education 4, no. 1 (2011): 107–125.

evidence.description: Analysis suggesting that word rarity and sentence length (text complexity) can effectively increase as students become more familiar with a topic within a coherent lesson unit.
evidence.source_reference: E.D. Hirsch, Why Knowledge Matters, Endnote 65

concepts.author_definition: The belief that education should be a 'natural unfolding' of the child's inherent nature and that content should be withheld if deemed 'developmentally inappropriate.'
The pedagogical principle that education should focus on general, transferable skills (like critical thinking) rather than specific subject-matter knowledge.
The belief that because every child is unique, education must be child-centered and guided by the child's unique interests and nature.
A label used to justify withholding certain academic topics from children based on the belief that their minds are not yet ready to process them.
concepts.common_alternative: A scientifically validated measure of a child's cognitive limits.
concepts.source_passage: Educators assert, for example, that Piaget has shown that some topics are “developmentally inappropriate” for children in a particular age group.

concepts.author_definition: A label used by educators to imply that certain academic topics are taught prematurely and should be withheld until a child reaches a certain maturational age.
concepts.common_alternative: The mainstream education view that instruction must align with a child's natural cognitive stages.
concepts.source_passage: To label a topic “developmentally inappropriate” implies that it is being taught prematurely, like feeding small children oysters Rockefeller when they should be eating oatmeal.

concepts.author_definition: The fourth stage of Piaget's theory, characterized by the ability to perform abstract logical reasoning independent of concrete experience.
concepts.common_alternative: Commonly viewed as a universal milestone of human cognitive maturity.
concepts.source_passage: Upon reaching that final stage, a mature child or an adult should be able to solve the following problem: If A is bigger than B, and B is bigger than C, is A bigger or smaller than C or the same?

concepts.author_definition: A state that is not a 'natural unfolding' but is instead determined by the external education and the knowledge base a child has acquired.
concepts.common_alternative: The belief that children must 'ripen' biologically before they can learn certain subjects.
concepts.source_passage: The stage of development—of readiness—is not entirely a natural unfolding.

concepts.author_definition: The hypothesis that any subject can be taught effectively in some intellectually honest form to any child at any stage of development.
The act of introducing children to the accumulated wisdom and facts known by successful citizens; essential for social equity.
concepts.common_alternative: Often critiqued by theorists as 'banking' education, cultural imperialism, or anti-individualistic.
concepts.source_passage: Transmission (introducing your child to what successful people in the society know and convey to their own children at home) is especially scorned by theorists today as being culturally imperialistic.

concepts.author_definition: The nationally shared knowledge of the public sphere and the standard language that allows citizens to participate equally in a democracy.
The overemphasis on innate, genetic components of learning and ability, which Hirsch argues is an implicit part of child-centered theories.
The educational overemphasis on innate, genetic components of learning at the expense of environmental factors like shared knowledge.
The belief, traced to Dewey, that education must be controlled at every point by the child's specific psychological capacities, interests, and habits.
A practice that attempts to match materials to each student's presumed ability level or 'learning style,' which Hirsch argues results in a 'dumbed down' and incoherent curriculum.
concepts.common_alternative: Tailoring teaching to meet individual student needs to ensure all students can reach learning goals.

concepts.author_definition: The specific, cumulative subject-matter knowledge that allows a student to make sense of new information and engage in hard work productively.
The act of a student reading a book on their own, which may be assigned by a teacher for communal discussion later, rather than just books they select themselves.
concepts.common_alternative: A period where children select and read books based solely on personal interest and 'leveled' difficulty.
concepts.source_passage: independent reading doesn’t necessarily mean that; it can also mean reading books on one’s own that have been assigned and are then later discussed communally

concepts.author_definition: A system where books are categorized by difficulty levels (often Lexile) and children are restricted to reading within their assigned level.
A system where books are rated by difficulty level based on technical formulas (sentence length/word rarity) and students are restricted to reading books at their 'correct' level.
The use of elaborate mathematical formulas and excessive decimal places to create an appearance of scientific exactitude where none exists.
The theory that reading comprehension is a general cognitive ability that can be applied equally to any text regardless of its subject matter.
concepts.common_alternative: Cognitive science suggests comprehension is domain-specific and dependent on background knowledge.

concepts.author_definition: The state of a curriculum where the lack of common, sequential content prevents teachers from knowing what students have already learned.
The language community and shared standard language (spoken and written) of the nation that allows individuals to communicate effectively with strangers.
Broadly includes speaking, listening, reading, and writing, all of which Hirsch argues are dependent on vocabulary and shared knowledge rather than just technical or emotional intelligence.
concepts.common_alternative: Often reduced to 'soft skills,' emotional intelligence, or technical proficiency like making PowerPoints.

concepts.author_definition: The cognitive principle that human skills are tied to specific subject-matter knowledge and do not transfer easily across different fields.
The possession of a broad and deep range of factual knowledge stored in long-term memory, which serves as the prerequisite for critical thinking.
An unfair educational paradox where students who already possess a large 'capital' of knowledge acquire new knowledge at an accelerating rate, while those with little knowledge fall further behind.
concepts.common_alternative: Often used in sociology to describe wealth accumulation, but here applied to cognitive and educational development.
concepts.source_passage: Education is bootstrapping, gov- erned by the “Matthew Effect,” that unfair paradox in the Book of Matthew: “For whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and he shall have more abundance: but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath.”

concepts.author_definition: Individuals who possess the requisite background knowledge and vocabulary to effectively utilize modern information tools like Google and digital libraries.
concepts.common_alternative: Often people assume anyone with 'digital literacy' or access to a computer can find information equally.
concepts.source_passage: Instead of being an agent of equality, Google rewards cognitive insiders. It consolidates educational inequality.

concepts.author_definition: The process of learning new vocabulary and its subtle connotations through context and exposure rather than explicit dictionary lookup.
concepts.source_passage: It has two defects: it is a slow, cumbersome mode of vocabulary building compared with implicit word learning, and it is a far less accurate mode of gaining the subtle connotations of words.

concepts.author_definition: Deep domain knowledge that experts use to perform tasks that novices mistake for general skill.
A distinction between the general skill of turning letters into sounds (decoding) and the domain-specific act of understanding meaning (comprehension).
The act of making hypotheses about word meanings and building meaning from both text and prior knowledge; specifically, NOT a general skill.
